chemistry.scope_summary: Anti-Markovnikov addition of thiyl radicals to alkenes. Used for decorating precision polymers with oligosaccharides. Good yields (71-80%) obtained with 10 minute residence times at 254 nm.

optimization_table.key_findings: 30 minute residence time maximized yield and minimized byproduct

light_source.position_relative_to_reactor: medium pressure mercury lamp wrapped around FEP tubing

figures.caption: Fig. 1.  The intensity of light (red line) decreases logarithmically with respect to path length (left), making ﬂow chemical systems more efﬁcient.

figures.flow_path: syringe to reactor chamber to product outlet
figures.key_information: UV photochemical flow reactor with detailed view showing UV light irradiating reaction mixture with molecular species in flow
figures.caption: Fig. 2.  Top: Schematic of the ﬂow system for the photo-induced cleavage of oligosaccharides from a resin. Bottom: The photolabile linker used. | Fig. 3.  By optimizing the residence time, product formation can be maxi- mized while product decomposition (from over-irradiation) can be minimized.

figures.trend: azide 8a decreases over time, 3H-azepinone initially increases then decreases, byproduct increases over time
figures.caption: Fig. 2.  Top: Schematic of the ﬂow system for the photo-induced cleavage of oligosaccharides from a resin. Bottom: The photolabile linker used. | Fig. 3.  By optimizing the residence time, product formation can be maxi- mized while product decomposition (from over-irradiation) can be minimized.

Fig. 4.  Byproduct formation was minimized for the photoelectrocyclization by careful control of the residence time. | Fig. 5.  Thiol-ene coupling was utilized for the efﬁcient and complete late-stage introduction of sugar moieties to our precision polymers.

Fig. 8.  A homemade photoreactor, irradiated on two sides by cold white LED lamps (visible light), was used to conduct ruthenium-catalyzed SET chemistry. | Fig. 9.  Our continuous ﬂow singlet oxygen generator has facilitated a number of transformations.

translation_logic.batch_limitation: Over-irradiation causing product decomposition and byproduct formation
translation_logic.flow_advantage: Better control of residence time to maximize product formation while minimizing decomposition
translation_logic.what_changed: Residence time optimization from minutes to precisely controlled exposure
translation_logic.what_stayed_same: UV irradiation wavelength and photochemical mechanism
translation_logic.safety_improvement: Reduced exposure to UV radiation and better heat dissipation

figure_descriptions: Reactor scheme showing UV photochemical flow reactor
Optimization plot showing residence time effects on product composition

conflicts: Pass2 mentions 254 nm absorption max and 10 min residence time in scope, but Pass3 shows 366 nm light source and 30 min optimal residence time
notes: Review paper discussing multiple photochemical transformations. Primary focus on residence time optimization to prevent over-irradiation. Limited quantitative data available as this is a review rather than experimental paper.
